1: bayyôm hahûʾ nāṯan hammeleḵ ʾAḥašwērôš ləʾEsṯēr hammalkāh ʾeṯ-bêṯ Hāmān ṣōrēr hayyəhûḏîm — 'On that day King Ahasuerus gave to Queen Esther the house of Haman the enemy of the Jews.' Bêṯ Hāmān (the house/estate of Haman) — the entire household property, which passed to the crown upon execution of a traitor. The appositional phrase ṣōrēr hayyəhûḏîm (enemy/adversary of the Jews, H6887) accompanies Haman's name as a permanent epithet; it appears first at 3:10 and recurs to ensure the reader reads every subsequent reference to Haman through this lens. ûMordoḵay bāʾ lipnê hammeleḵ kî-higgîḏāh ʾEsṯēr māh hûʾ-lāh — 'Mordecai came before the king, for Esther had told what he was to her.' The disclosure of the kinship relationship (Esther's cousin/guardian) is now made explicit to the king for the first time.
2: wayyāsar hammeleḵ ʾeṯ-ṭabbaʿtô ʾăšer hĕʿĕḇîr mēHāmān wayyittənāh ləMordoḵay — 'The king removed his signet ring (ṭabbaʿat, H2885) which he had taken back from Haman and gave it to Mordecai.' The signet ring is the instrument of royal authority — Haman received it to seal the destruction decree (3:10); now Mordecai receives it as the king's authorized agent. The transfer of the ring formally transfers Haman's position. wataśem ʾEsṯēr ʾeṯ-Mordoḵay ʿal-bêṯ Hāmān — 'and Esther set Mordecai over the house of Haman.' The estate granted by the king is placed under Mordecai's administration by Esther — a double authority structure: the king grants to Esther, Esther delegates to Mordecai, completing the reversal from the sequence in chapter 3 where Haman received the king's ring and authority.
3: watṯōsep̄ ʾEsṯēr watḏabbēr lip̄nê hammeleḵ wattippōl lip̄nê raglāyw watteḇk — 'Esther spoke again before the king and fell at his feet and wept.' Wattippōl lip̄nê raglāyw (fell before his feet) — prostration is the most supplicatory posture possible, more than the extended scepter approach of 5:2. The weeping (bāḵāh, H1058) is not performed but genuine: the decree that threatens her people remains in force despite Haman's death. ûtəḥannen-lô ləhaʿăḇîr ʾeṯ-rāʿaṯ Hāmān hāʾăgāgî wəʾeṯ-maḥăšaḇtô ʾăšer ḥāšaḇ ʿal-hayyəhûḏîm — 'and implored him to put away the evil of Haman the Agagite and the plan that he had planned against the Jews.' Ḥāšaḇ (H2803 = to plan, reckon, think): the plan (maḥăšebet) that Haman had thought out is now to be overturned. The Agagite epithet (H91) connects Haman to Agag king of Amalek (1 Sam 15) — locating the conflict within the long Amalekite-Israelite enmity.
4: wayyôšeṭ hammeleḵ ləʾEsṯēr ʾeṯ-šarḇîṭ hazzāhāḇ — 'The king extended to Esther the golden scepter.' The extension of the golden scepter (H8275, the same act as 5:2) signals that the queen's unsummoned approach is accepted again. The repetition of the scepter scene emphasizes that Esther's access to the king is never guaranteed — each entry into the inner court is an act of petition requiring royal acceptance. watāqam ʾEsṯēr wattaʿămod lip̄nê hammeleḵ — 'and Esther arose and stood before the king.' The movement from prostration (v3) to standing (v4) enacts the transition from mourning petition to formal request.
5: watōʾmer ʾim-ʿal-hammeleḵ ṭôḇ wəʾim-māṣāʾṯî ḥēn ləp̄ānāyw — 'If it please the king and if I have found favor before him.' Esther's third banquet-approach formula, stacking conditions. wənākôn haddāḇār lip̄nê hammeleḵ wəṭôḇāh ʾănî bəʿênāyw — 'and the thing seems right to the king and I am good in his eyes.' The fourfold condition — king's pleasure, found favor, right in his estimation, good in his sight — represents maximum deference in Persian court protocol. yikkāṯēḇ ləhāšîḇ ʾeṯ-hassəp̄ārîm maḥăšeḇeṯ Hāmān ben-Hammedāṯāʾ hāʾăgāgî ʾăšer kāṯaḇ ləʾabbēḏ ʾeṯ-hayyəhûḏîm ʾăšer bəḵol-məḏînôṯ hammeleḵ — 'let it be written to revoke the letters of the plan of Haman ... which he wrote to destroy the Jews in all the king's provinces.' Lhāšîḇ (H7725, hiphil = to cause to return, revoke) — the request is not merely to nullify but to return/recall the letters; whether a Persian decree could be formally overturned is the legal complexity Esther is navigating.
6: kî êḵāḵāh ʾûḵal wərāʾîṯî bərāʿāh ʾăšer-yimṣāʾ ʾeṯ-ʿammî — 'For how can I bear to see the disaster that will come upon my people?' Bərāʿāh (H7451 = evil, disaster, calamity) — the word that will befall her people is rāʿāh, the covenant-disaster term. wəêḵāḵāh ʾûḵal wərāʾîṯî bəʾăḇōḏan môladtî — 'and how can I bear to see the destruction of my kindred (môladtî)?' Môledet (H4138 = birth, lineage, kindred — those born from the same family line) is the kinship word: Esther's appeal is not abstract but personal and genealogical. The doubled rhetorical question (êḵāḵāh ʾûḵal + wəêḵāḵāh ʾûḵal) intensifies the emotional impossibility of passive acceptance.
7: wayyōʾmer hammeleḵ ʾAḥašwērôš ləʾEsṯēr hammalkāh ûləMordoḵay hayyəhûḏî — 'King Ahasuerus said to Queen Esther and to Mordecai the Jew.' The king now addresses Esther and Mordecai together — the official couple in his administration. hinnēh bêṯ Hāmān nāṯattî ləʾEsṯēr wəʾôṯô tālû ʿal-hāʿēṣ — 'Behold, I have given the house of Haman to Esther, and they have hanged him on the gallows.' The king rehearses what has already been done — the estate given, the man executed — to frame what cannot yet be done: overturning a sealed royal decree. taḥat ʾăšer šālaḥ yāḏô bayyəhûḏîm — 'because he sent his hand against the Jews' — the idiom šālaḥ yāḏ (sent the hand) denoting violent action (cf. Neh 13:21).
8: wəʾattem kiṯḇû ʿal-hayyəhûḏîm kaṭṭôḇ bəʿênêḵem bəšem hammeleḵ — 'And you, write about the Jews as seems good to you in the name of the king.' The authorization is given to Mordecai and Esther to compose a second decree, but the structural problem is stated explicitly: kî-kiṯāḇ ʾăšer-niḵtaḇ bəšem-hammeleḵ wəneḥtôm bəṭabbaʿat hammeleḵ ʾên ləhāšîḇ — 'for an edict written in the king's name and sealed with the king's ring cannot be revoked.' The Persian legal principle of the irrevocability of sealed royal decrees (cf. Dan 6:8,12,15) creates the legal constraint: the original decree cannot be nullified, only countered. The new decree will authorize the Jews to defend themselves, effectively rendering the original decree ineffective in practice without formally rescinding it.
9: wayyiqqārəʾû sōp̄ərê hammeleḵ bāʿēṯ hahîʾ baḥōḏeš haššəlîšî hûʾ-ḥōḏeš sîwān — 'The king's scribes were summoned at that time in the third month, which is the month of Sivan.' The third month Sivan (May–June) is approximately two months after Nisan/Abib (month 1) when Haman cast lots (3:7). The date is significant: on the 23rd of Sivan, the counter-decree is issued. wayyikkāṯēḇ kəḵol-ʾăšer-ṣiwwāh Mordoḵay ʾel-hayyəhûḏîm wəʾel hāʾăḥašdarpənîm wəhappaḥôṯ wəśārê hammĕḏînôṯ ʾăšer mēhōddû wəʿaḏ-kûš — 'and it was written according to all that Mordecai commanded, to the Jews and to the satraps and governors and officials of the provinces from India to Ethiopia.' The administrative reach (127 provinces, 1:1) mirrors the scope of the original threat decree (3:12-14).
10: wayyiḵtōḇ bəšēm hammeleḵ ʾAḥašwērôš wayyaḥtōm bəṭabbaʿat hammeleḵ — 'He wrote in the name of King Ahasuerus and sealed it with the king's ring.' The parallel to 3:12 (wayyikkāṯēḇ/wayyaḥtōm) makes the formal equivalence explicit: Mordecai's counter-decree has exactly the same legal weight as Haman's original decree, sealed with the same ring. wayyišlaḥ səp̄ārîm bəyaḏ hārāṣîm bassûsîm rōḵəḇê hārekeš hāʾăḥaštranim bənê hārammāḵîm — 'and sent letters by couriers on horseback, on swift horses bred from the royal stud.' The repeated emphasis on the speed of the horses (H7409 + H7424 = relay-horse breeds of the Persian royal postal system, the angareion) signals urgency: the counter-decree must reach every province before the original.
11: ʾăšer nāṯan hammeleḵ layyəhûḏîm ʾăšer bəḵol-ʿîr wāʿîr lihhāqāhēl wəlaʿămōḏ ʿal-napšām — 'That the king permitted the Jews who were in every city to assemble and stand for their lives.' Lihāqāhēl (H6950, niphal = to assemble, gather together) and laʿămōḏ ʿal-napšām (to stand for their lives/selves) are the two rights granted: assembly and self-defense. The new decree does not cancel the old but creates a counter-authorization: the Jews may do to those who attack them exactly what the original decree authorized others to do to the Jews. lhašmîḏ wlaharōg ûlaʾabbēḏ — 'to destroy, to kill, and to annihilate' — the exact three-term formula of 3:13, now applied in reverse direction. The narrative irony: the language of intended genocide becomes the language of authorized defense.
12: bəyôm ʾeḥāḏ bəḵol-məḏînôṯ hammeleḵ ʾAḥašwērôš bišlōšāh ʿāśār ləḥōḏeš šənêm ʿāśār hûʾ-ḥōḏeš ʾăḏār — 'On one day in all the provinces of King Ahasuerus, on the thirteenth of the twelfth month, which is the month of Adar.' The specific date — 13 Adar — is fixed by Haman's lot-casting from 3:7 (which determined the month of Adar as the time for the destruction). The date thus remains unchanged: only the nature of the event on that day has been reversed by the counter-decree. Both decrees converge on the same calendar day: the day Haman selected for the Jews' destruction becomes the day of Jewish survival and victory.
13: paṯšegen hakkəṯāḇ lhinnāṯen dāṯ bəḵol-məḏînāh ûməḏînāh — 'A copy of the writing was to be issued as a law in every province.' Paṯšegen (H6572) is a Persian loanword (Old Persian *patiša-ka = copy/transcript) used in Esther 3:14 and 4:8 as well — the formal administrative term for the distributed copies of the royal decree. gālûy ləḵol-hāʿammîm wlihyôṯ hayyəhûḏîm ʿăṯûḏîm layyôm hazzeh — 'proclaimed to all peoples so that the Jews would be ready on that day.' ʿĀṯûḏ (H6264 = ready, prepared) — the Jews are authorized to prepare themselves militarily. The counter-decree produces readiness rather than vulnerability.
14: hārāṣîm rōḵəḇê hārekeš hāʾăḥaštranim yaṣəʾû məbōhālîm ûḏəḥûpîm bidiḇar hammeleḵ — 'The couriers riding the swift royal horses went out hurriedly and pressed on by the king's command.' Məbōhālîm (H926, pual = hurried, rushed) and dəḥûpîm (H1765, qal = pressed/urged forward) together depict the maximum speed of the Persian postal system. wəhaddāṯ nittənāh bəšûšan habbîrāh — 'and the edict was issued in Susa the citadel.' The decree flows outward from the capital to the whole empire — the same spatial movement as 3:15.
15: ûMordoḵay yāṣāʾ millip̄nê hammeleḵ biliḇûš malkûṯ tēḵēlet wāḥûr wăʿăṭeret zāhāḇ gəḏôlāh wətakrîḵ bûṣ wəʾargāmān — 'Mordecai went out from before the king in royal clothing of blue and white, and with a great golden crown, and a garment of fine linen and purple.' The colors — tēḵēlet (H8504 = blue-violet, the color of the priestly garments, Exod 25:4) and wāḥûr (H2353 = white, fine white linen) — are not merely Persian court colors but carry the association of priestly and tabernacle textiles. Mordecai's public appearance in royal garments is the full reversal of 4:1 where he wore sackcloth and ashes. wəhāʿîr šûšān ṣāhălāh wəśāmēḥāh — 'and the city of Susa shouted for joy and was glad.' The city that was confused and disturbed at the original decree (3:15 — wəhāʿîr šûšān nāḇôḵāh) is now rejoicing.
16: layyəhûḏîm hāyəṯāh ʾôrāh wəśimḥāh wəśāśōn wîqār — 'For the Jews there was light and gladness and joy and honor.' The four-word summary of the reversal uses a vocabulary of blessing: ʾôrāh (H219 = light — the metaphor for divine favor and life, Ps 27:1), śimḥāh (H8057 = gladness), śāśōn (H8342 = joy, the word used in the great joy of the feasts), wîqār (H3366 = honor, worth). These four nouns are the positive reversal of the four-word destruction formula from 3:13 (lhašmîḏ lahărōg ûləʾabbēḏ). The word-for-word opposition is structurally deliberate: destruction/killing/annihilation/plunder → light/gladness/joy/honor.
17: ûḇəḵol-məḏînāh ûməḏînāh ûḇəḵol-ʿîr wāʿîr məqôm ʾăšer diḇar-hammeleḵ wədāṯô maggîaʿ śimḥāh wəśāśōn layyəhûḏîm mišṯeh wəyôm ṭôḇ — 'In each and every province and city, wherever the king's edict and command reached, there was gladness and joy among the Jews, a feast and a holiday.' The repetition of the distribution formula — every province, every city — mirrors the distribution of the first decree (3:14-15) to emphasize that the reversal is as total and complete as the original threat. wərabbîm mēʿammê hāʾāreṣ miṯyahăḏîm kî-nāpal paḥaḏ-hayyəhûḏîm ʿălêhem — 'and many of the peoples of the land declared themselves Jews (miṯyahăḏîm, H3054), because fear of the Jews had fallen upon them.' Hiṯyahēḏ is a hapax — the unique denominative verb meaning 'to Judaize, declare oneself a Jew.' The fear (paḥaḏ, H6343) of the Jews is the reversal of the Jews' fear: those who feared now cause fear.
